lesson.title: Testing
lesson.summary: Write automated tests to verify your code works correctly.

sections.heading: Your first test
sections.bodyHtml:
Test files end in _test.go and live alongside your code. A test function takes *testing.T and starts with Test:

// main.go
func Add(a, b int) int {
    return a + b
}

// main_test.go
package main

import "testing"

func TestAdd(t *testing.T) {
    got := Add(2, 3)
    want := 5
    if got != want {
        t.Errorf("Add(2, 3) = %d; want %d", got, want)
    }
}

sections.heading: Running tests
sections.bodyHtml:
go test                    # run tests in current package
go test -v                # verbose, shows each test name
go test ./...             # run all tests in all packages
go test -run TestAdd      # run only tests matching pattern

Green output means all pass. Red means failures. Common flags:

go test -count=1   # disable cache (force re-run)
go test -bench=.  # run benchmarks

sections.heading: Table-driven tests
sections.bodyHtml:
Test multiple cases with a table. This is the idiomatic Go style:

func TestAdd(t *testing.T) {
    tests := []struct {
        a, b, want int
    }{
        {2, 3, 5},
        {0, 0, 0},
        {-1, 1, 0},
        {100, -50, 50},
    }
    for _, tt := range tests {
        got := Add(tt.a, tt.b)
        if got != tt.want {
            t.Errorf("Add(%d, %d) = %d; want %d", tt.a, tt.b, got, tt.want)
        }
    }
}

Add new cases by adding a row to the table — no code duplication.

sections.heading: Your turn
sections.bodyHtml:
Open practice/testing/main_test.go.

Write a table-driven test for a Max(a, b int) int function that returns the larger of two integers.

exercises.title: Table-driven test for Max
exercises.instructions: Implement Max(a, b int) int and write table-driven tests covering positive, negative, and equal cases.
